messages.content: You are a senior HVAC knowledge-base reviewer. Validate extracted knowledge objects from one equipment manual. Accept only useful, grounded, reviewable operational knowledge supported by the evidence. Reject marketing claims, UI-only navigation, isolated terminal labels, pure part names, duplicate/noisy items, generic prose with no operational value, or items whose evidence does not support the structured payload. Supported knowledge_type values: fault_code, parameter_spec, performance_spec, maintenance_procedure, application_guidance, operational_sequence, fault_diagnostic_rule, diagnostic_step, symptom. Reply with strict JSON only.

Manual filename: auto_ashrae2019.pdf
Equipment class: cooling_tower

Review every candidate below. Preserve candidate_id exactly.
Allowed category_if_not values: null, marketing, ui_behavior, terminal_label, part_name, duplicate, unsupported_type, weak_evidence, other.

{"candidates": [{"candidate_id": "cand_371a63118a016f19", "knowledge_type": "parameter_spec", "title": "Warehouse Ventilation Rate", "summary": "ASHRAE Standard 62.1 recommends 0.3 L/(s·m²) as a design criterion for warehouse ventilation.", "structured_payload": {"parameter_name": "warehouse_ventilation_rate", "title": "Warehouse Ventilation Rate", "summary": "ASHRAE Standard 62.1 recommends 0.3 L/(s·m²) as a design criterion for warehouse ventilation."}, "evidence": "Licensed for single user. © 2019 ASHRAE, Inc.3.8 2019 ASHRAE Handbook—HVAC Applications (SI)
Cruise Terminals. Ventilation design must ensure that fumes
and odors from forklifts and cargo in work areas do not penetrate
occupied and administrative areas.
Bus Terminals. The primary concerns with enclosed bus loading
areas are health and safety probl ems, which must be handled by
proper ventilation (see Chapter 16). Although diesel  engine fumes
are generally not as noxious as ga soline fumes, bus terminals often
have many buses loading and unloading at the same time, and thetotal amount of fumes and odors may be disturbing.
In terms of health and safety, enclosed bus loading areas and
automobile parking garages pres ent the most serious problems.
Three major problems are encountered , the first and most serious of
which is emission of carbon monoxide (CO) by cars and oxides of
nitrogen (NO
x) by buses, which can caus e serious illness and pos-
sibly death.", "source_pages": [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6], "trust_level": "L4"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_106a1a90d503b874", "knowledge_type": "performance_spec", "title": "Air Curtain Effectiveness Range", "summary": "Air curtains have a range of effectiveness from 60 to 90%, depending on type and application.", "structured_payload": {"parameter_name": "air_curtain_effectiveness_range", "title": "Air Curtain Effectiveness Range", "summary": "Air curtains have a range of effectiveness from 60 to 90%, depending on type and application."}, "evidence": "It is represented as a percent-
age, and the amount of energy sa ved is reduced by the energy con-
sumed by the unit. Research (e.g ., Pappas and Tassou [2003]) shows
that air curtains have a range of effectiveness from 60 to 90%,
depending on the type and application.
To fully realize benefi ts of using an air curtain unit, make sure
that equipment is prope rly sized, installed, adjusted, and main-
tained.
Application Considerations
Air curtain unit selection depe nds on the opening’s width and
height. To maximize effectiveness, the air curtain unit must at leastcover or slightly overlap the entire opening, and have a minimum
velocity projection of 2 m/s at the target surface (Wang and Zhong
2014). The unit is usually mounted above or beside a door or windowopening. When mounted above the opening, the horizontal air cur-
tain unit discharges its air verti cally down across the opening.", "source_pages": [6], "trust_level": "L3"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_ca22afdb66f21864", "knowledge_type": "parameter_spec", "title": "Air Curtain Minimum Velocity Projection", "summary": "Air curtain unit must have a minimum velocity projection of 2 m/s at the target surface.", "structured_payload": {"parameter_name": "air_curtain_min_velocity_projection", "title": "Air Curtain Minimum Velocity Projection", "summary": "Air curtain unit must have a minimum velocity projection of 2 m/s at the target surface."}, "evidence": "It is represented as a percent-
age, and the amount of energy sa ved is reduced by the energy con-
sumed by the unit. Research (e.g ., Pappas and Tassou [2003]) shows
that air curtains have a range of effectiveness from 60 to 90%,
depending on the type and application.
To fully realize benefi ts of using an air curtain unit, make sure
that equipment is prope rly sized, installed, adjusted, and main-
tained.
Application Considerations
Air curtain unit selection depe nds on the opening’s width and
height. To maximize effectiveness, the air curtain unit must at leastcover or slightly overlap the entire opening, and have a minimum
velocity projection of 2 m/s at the target surface (Wang and Zhong
2014). The unit is usually mounted above or beside a door or windowopening. When mounted above the opening, the horizontal air cur-
tain unit discharges its air verti cally down across the opening.", "source_pages": [6], "trust_level": "L3"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_6c31a183295afe1f", "knowledge_type": "application_guidance", "title": "Perchloric Acid Exhaust System Material", "summary": "Ducts for perchloric acid exhaust should be constructed of stainless steel with chromium and nickel content not less than 316L, or suitable nonmetallic material.", "structured_payload": {"parameter_name": "perchloric_acid_exhaust_duct_material", "title": "Perchloric Acid Exhaust System Material", "summary": "Ducts for perchloric acid exhaust should be constructed of stainless steel with chromium and nickel content not less than 316L, or suitable nonmetallic material."}, "evidence": "Ducts should be c onstructed of a stainless steel
material, with a chromium and nickel content not less than that of
316L stainless steel, or of a suit able nonmetallic material. Joints
should be welded and ground smooth . A perchloric acid exhaust
system should only be used for work involving perchloric acid.
Application : Process and research la boratories using perchloric
acid. Mandatory use becaus e of explosion hazard.
California.  Special hood with sash openi ngs on multiple sides (u su-
ally horizontal).
Applic ation : For enclosing large and complex research apparatus
that require access from two or more sides.
Floor-Mounted Hood (Walk-In).  Hood with sash openings to the
floor. Sash can be either horizontal or vertical.
Application : For enclosing large or complex research apparatus.
Not designed for personnel to enter while operations are in prog-
ress.
Distillation.", "source_pages": [2], "trust_level": "L3"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_8d682845565b4b71", "knowledge_type": "application_guidance", "title": "Perchloric Acid Exhaust System Washdown", "summary": "Perchloric acid exhaust system must be equipped with an internal water washdown and drainage system.", "structured_payload": {"parameter_name": "perchloric_acid_exhaust_washdown", "title": "Perchloric Acid Exhaust System Washdown", "summary": "Perchloric acid exhaust system must be equipped with an internal water washdown and drainage system."}, "evidence": "Hood with special integr al work surfaces, coved
corners, and nonorganic lining ma terials. Perchloric acid is anextremely active oxidizing agent.  Its vapors can form unstable
deposits in the ductwork that present a potential explosion haz-
ard. To alleviate this hazard, the exhaust system must beequipped with an internal water washdown and drainage system,
and the ductwork must be constructed of smooth, impervious,cleanable materials that are resistant to acid attack. The internal
washdown system must completely flush the ductwork, exhaust
fan, discharge stack, and fume  hood inner surfaces. Ductwork
should be kept as short as poss ible with minimum elbows. Per-
chloric acid exhaust systems wi th longer duct runs may need a
zoned washdown system to avoid water flow rates in excess of
the capacity to drain water from the hood. Because perchloric
acid is an extremely active oxidizing agent, organic materialsshould not be used in the exhaust system in places such as joints
and gaskets.", "source_pages": [2], "trust_level": "L3"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_1f33afe212399482", "knowledge_type": "application_guidance", "title": "Fume Hood Sash Bypass Prevention", "summary": "Fume hoods with vertically rising sash sections should include provisions around the sash to prevent bypass of ceiling plenum air.", "structured_payload": {"parameter_name": "fume_hood_sash_bypass_prevention", "title": "Fume Hood Sash Bypass Prevention", "summary": "Fume hoods with vertically rising sash sections should include provisions around the sash to prevent bypass of ceiling plenum air."}, "evidence": "A horiz ontally operable
sash incorporates multiple panels that slide in multiple tracks,
allowing the open area to be positioned across the face of the hood.
The combination of a horizontally operable sash m ounted within a
single vertically operabl e sash section allows the entire hood face to
be opened for setup. The opening area  can then be limited by closing
the vertical panel, with only the horizontally sliding sash sections
used during experimentati on. Both the multiple vertical sash section
and combination sash arrangement allow the use of larger fumehoods with limited openi ng areas, resulting in reduced exhaust air-
flow requirements. Fume  hoods with vertically rising sash sections
should include provisions around the sash to prevent the bypass of
ceiling plenum air into the fume hood. Fig. 1 Bypass Fume Hood  with Vertical Sash
and Bypass Air Inlet

EDUFIRE.IR.", "source_pages": [2], "trust_level": "L3"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_a00f3da698438d02", "knowledge_type": "application_guidance", "title": "Cultural Heritage Loading Dock Pressurization", "summary": "Loading docks should ideally be positively pressurized, and shared walls with collections zones should be carefully designed.", "structured_payload": {"parameter_name": "loading_dock_pressurization", "title": "Cultural Heritage Loading Dock Pressurization", "summary": "Loading docks should ideally be positively pressurized, and shared walls with collections zones should be carefully designed."}, "evidence": "Appr opriate zoning should minimize the
necessity for downstream subzone equipment (VAV/reheat, subzone
humidification) in most occupied  and unoccupied collections zones.
Activities that pose a potential threat to collections environments
(food areas, loading docks, fabr ication and rough shops, mainte -
nance and housekeeping, etc.) should be kept away from collec -
tions zones, with exhaust from  these areas located away and
downwind from any fresh air inta kes for collections systems and
the rest of the building. One key exception to this practice may be
relative-humidity-controlled crate storage, which may share a zone
with occupied collections areas.  Loading docks should ideally be
positively pressurized, and shared  walls with collections zones
should be carefully designed to mitigate any potential energy trans -
fer, whether from a load perspective or to red uce risk of condensation
or other issues.", "source_pages": [3], "trust_level": "L3"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_da932520c68e7a05", "knowledge_type": "application_guidance", "title": "Cultural Heritage Occupancy Density for Storage Zones", "summary": "Unoccupied storage zones may have 100 m² per person or less.", "structured_payload": {"parameter_name": "storage_zone_occupancy_density", "title": "Cultural Heritage Occupancy Density for Storage Zones", "summary": "Unoccupied storage zones may have 100 m² per person or less."}, "evidence": "Though common when sp ecifying some systems types
such as four-pipe subcool/reheat and desiccant designs, adequate
latent capacity is often disregarded with two-pipe and standard pack-
age unit designs, resulting in envir onments that may be able to main-
tain temperature control, but commonly see relative humidity
conditions rise beyond safe limits as exterior temperatures climb and
cooling capacity is dedicated to the sensible load.
Certain specific load characteristics of cultural heritage build -
ings should be considered in syst em design. Occupa ncy patterns can
vary widely among zones. Noncol lections zones and occupied col -
lections zones tend to have hig h occupancy only at certain times,
which may include events outside  of normal operating hours. Some
gallery occupancies are as high as 1 m
2 per person, but unoccupied
storage zones may have 100 m2 per person or less. Systems should
be designed to handle maximum load s as well as the more common
part loads.", "source_pages": [3], "trust_level": "L3"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_4f82216c27f5a69b", "knowledge_type": "application_guidance", "title": "Cultural Heritage Gallery Occupancy Density", "summary": "Some gallery occupancies are as high as 1 m² per person.", "structured_payload": {"parameter_name": "gallery_occupancy_density", "title": "Cultural Heritage Gallery Occupancy Density", "summary": "Some gallery occupancies are as high as 1 m² per person."}, "evidence": "Though common when sp ecifying some systems types
such as four-pipe subcool/reheat and desiccant designs, adequate
latent capacity is often disregarded with two-pipe and standard pack-
age unit designs, resulting in envir onments that may be able to main-
tain temperature control, but commonly see relative humidity
conditions rise beyond safe limits as exterior temperatures climb and
cooling capacity is dedicated to the sensible load.
Certain specific load characteristics of cultural heritage build -
ings should be considered in syst em design. Occupa ncy patterns can
vary widely among zones. Noncol lections zones and occupied col -
lections zones tend to have hig h occupancy only at certain times,
which may include events outside  of normal operating hours. Some
gallery occupancies are as high as 1 m
2 per person, but unoccupied
storage zones may have 100 m2 per person or less. Systems should
be designed to handle maximum load s as well as the more common
part loads.", "source_pages": [3], "trust_level": "L3"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_4377a13d46cfbdcb", "knowledge_type": "application_guidance", "title": "Cultural Heritage Design Occupancy Assumption", "summary": "Many engineers design to 2 m² per person because part of the room is never occupied.", "structured_payload": {"parameter_name": "design_occupancy_assumption", "title": "Cultural Heritage Design Occupancy Assumption", "summary": "Many engineers design to 2 m² per person because part of the room is never occupied."}, "evidence": "Many engineers design to 2 m2 per person because part
of the room is never occupied. In zones where spaces are extensively
used for receptions, openings, and other high-traffic activities, even
higher density assumptions may be  justified. Continual and close

EDUFIRE.IR.", "source_pages": [3], "trust_level": "L3"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_7a4e13f706b03eae", "knowledge_type": "application_guidance", "title": "Cultural Heritage Envelope Performance Reassessment", "summary": "Where envelope performance is in question, first reassess environmental goals: consider higher or lower relative humidity ranges to minimize vapor pressure differences.", "structured_payload": {"parameter_name": "envelope_performance_reassessment", "title": "Cultural Heritage Envelope Performance Reassessment", "summary": "Where envelope performance is in question, first reassess environmental goals: consider higher or lower relative humidity ranges to minimize vapor pressure differences."}, "evidence": "Where possible,  a separate loading dock may be
specified for cleaner transfer of collections materials and crates, with
direct access to crate storage and collections intake work areas.
System Design and Envelope Performance
System designs that ca ll for interior moisture control, whether
humidification or dehum idification, must carefully consider the
likely performance of the existi ng or designed envelope for both
thermal insulation and vapor tran sfer. These issues are commonly
discussed for exterior envelopes, but the same issues can exist with
excessive thermal or vapor differentials across interior walls.
Thermal insulation/barriers should be adequate to moderate heat
transfer through the structure, with  particular attention paid to ther -
mal bridges (e.g., wall studs, fl oor/wall junctions, roof/wall junc -
tions) that may be sources of heat  gain/lo ss or potential condensation
points.
Vapor incursion or loss occurs by two primar y paths: air  leakage/
gaps and diffusion.", "source_pages": [3, 4, 6], "trust_level": "L4"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_9d57fa510108cf3d", "knowledge_type": "application_guidance", "title": "Cultural Heritage Microenvironment Alternatives", "summary": "Alternatives for containment of preservation environments include microenvironments, offsite purpose-built storage, and box-in-box solutions.", "structured_payload": {"parameter_name": "preservation_environment_alternatives", "title": "Cultural Heritage Microenvironment Alternatives", "summary": "Alternatives for containment of preservation environments include microenvironments, offsite purpose-built storage, and box-in-box solutions."}, "evidence": "Good manufacturing
practices (GMP) may be required.
 Liquid storage (fuel and nonprop ellants), flammable and com-
bustible storage, radioactive mate rial storage, hazardous chem-
ical storage, and ammunition storage.
 Automated storage and retrieva l systems (AS/RS), which are
designed for maximum storage and minimum personnel on site.
They are built for lower-temperature operation with minimalheat and light needed, but require a tall structure with extremely
level floors. In some cases,  specialty HVAC  equipment is
required for servers and other comp uter areas in AS/RS facility.
Features already now common in  warehouse designs are higher
bays, sophisticated ma terials-handling equipment, broadband con-
nectivity access, and more distri bution networks. A wide range of
storage alternatives, picking alte rnatives, material-handling equip-
ment, and software exist to meet the physical and operationalrequirements.", "source_pages": [1, 3], "trust_level": "L4"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_5f9a706e38ca2c6e", "knowledge_type": "application_guidance", "title": "Cultural Heritage Modulating Dampers Fail Closed", "summary": "Modulating outdoor air dampers may be set to fail closed to minimize infiltration during interrupted operation.", "structured_payload": {"parameter_name": "modulating_dampers_fail_closed", "title": "Cultural Heritage Modulating Dampers Fail Closed", "summary": "Modulating outdoor air dampers may be set to fail closed to minimize infiltration during interrupted operation."}, "evidence": "Licensed for single user. © 2019 ASHRAE, Inc.3.8 2019 ASHRAE Handbook—HVAC Applications (SI)
Cruise Terminals. Ventilation design must ensure that fumes
and odors from forklifts and cargo in work areas do not penetrate
occupied and administrative areas.
Bus Terminals. The primary concerns with enclosed bus loading
areas are health and safety probl ems, which must be handled by
proper ventilation (see Chapter 16). Although diesel  engine fumes
are generally not as noxious as ga soline fumes, bus terminals often
have many buses loading and unloading at the same time, and thetotal amount of fumes and odors may be disturbing.
In terms of health and safety, enclosed bus loading areas and
automobile parking garages pres ent the most serious problems.
Three major problems are encountered , the first and most serious of
which is emission of carbon monoxide (CO) by cars and oxides of
nitrogen (NO
x) by buses, which can caus e serious illness and pos-
sibly death.", "source_pages": [1, 2, 3], "trust_level": "L4"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_7bd7eb06284e9774", "knowledge_type": "application_guidance", "title": "Cultural Heritage Latent Capacity Consideration", "summary": "Adequate latent capacity is often disregarded with two-pipe and standard package unit designs, resulting in environments that may maintain temperature but see relative humidity rise beyond safe limits.", "structured_payload": {"parameter_name": "latent_capacity_consideration", "title": "Cultural Heritage Latent Capacity Consideration", "summary": "Adequate latent capacity is often disregarded with two-pipe and standard package unit designs, resulting in environments that may maintain temperature but see relative humidity rise beyond safe limits."}, "evidence": "WAREHOUSES AND DISTRIBUTION CENTERS
General Design Considerations
Warehouses can be defined as facilities that provide proper envi-
ronment for the purpose of storing goods and materials. They are
also used to store equipment and material inventory at industrialfacilities. At times, warehouses  may be open to the public. The
buildings are generally not air conditioned, but often have sufficient
heat and ventilation to provide a tolerable working environment. In
many cases, associated facilities occupied by office workers, such as
shipping, receiving, and inventor y control offices, are air condi-
tioned. Warehouses must be designed to accommodate the loads of
materials to be stored, associated  handling equipmen t, receiving and
shipping operations and associated trucking, and needs of operating
personnel.", "source_pages": [1, 2, 3, 6], "trust_level": "L4"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_df7b3acb099344ff", "knowledge_type": "parameter_spec", "title": "Ceiling/Plenum/Room Attenuation for Mineral Fiber Tile", "summary": "Attenuation in dB for mineral fiber tile (4.9 kg/m², 16 mm) at 63 Hz is 13 dB.", "structured_payload": {"parameter_name": "mineral_fiber_tile_attenuation_63Hz", "title": "Ceiling/Plenum/Room Attenuation for Mineral Fiber Tile", "summary": "Attenuation in dB for mineral fiber tile (4.9 kg/m², 16 mm) at 63 Hz is 13 dB."}, "evidence": "Note that silencerspreferably should be inserted at the penetration of the
mechanical equipment room (MER) walls or externalbuilding elements to minimize breakout before the
silencer.
(c) Consider increasing the length of ductwork or introducing
bends or a plenum to increa se the sound attenuation. Care
needs to be taken to ensure that the breakout noise path(s)
are still acceptable where additional ductwork is intro-
duced.
